title: Audit Evidence — Collecting, Evaluating, and Presenting Audit Proof

sections.title: What Makes Good Audit Evidence
sections.content:
An auditor's job is to verify that controls are designed effectively and operating as intended. Every control they test requires evidence — proof that the control exists, has been implemented correctly, and is producing the intended results. Without evidence, a control does not exist in the eyes of an auditor, regardless of how well it is designed on paper. Understanding what constitutes good evidence is the foundation of audit readiness.

**What is audit evidence?** Any information used by the auditor to determine whether controls meet audit criteria. It can take many forms — documents, system logs, interview notes, configuration screenshots, or direct observation. For evidence to be useful, it must meet four criteria, collectively known as the SRRT framework:

**Sufficient** — enough evidence to support the conclusion. A single access review report from one month provides limited evidence, but quarterly reports covering the full audit period (typically 12 months) provide sufficient evidence of consistent performance. The quantity of evidence should match the risk: higher-risk controls require more evidence samples than lower-risk ones.

**Reliable** — from a trustworthy source. Evidence collected directly by the auditor (through observation or independent system access) is more reliable than evidence provided by the auditee. Original documents are more reliable than copies. System-generated audit logs are more reliable than manually created screenshots. The general principle: the closer the evidence is to the source, the more reliable it is.

**Relevant** — directly related to the audit objective. Evidence that does not address the specific control being tested is not useful, even if it is sufficient and reliable. A password policy document is relevant to testing access control requirements but irrelevant to testing incident response procedures. Relevance should be determined before evidence collection begins.

**Timely** — current enough to support the conclusion. Evidence from last year's audit does not demonstrate this year's control operation. For controls that operate continuously (like access controls), evidence from throughout the audit period is needed. For point-in-time controls (like annual risk assessments), the evidence should be from the current period.

**The evidence hierarchy — ranked by reliability:**
| Level | Type | Example | Reliability |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| 1st | Direct observation | Auditor watches an access review performed | Highest |
| 2nd | System-generated | Audit logs, system reports, automated attestation | High |
| 3rd | Documentary | Policies, procedures, signed forms | Medium |
| 4th | Analytical | Trend analysis, benchmark comparisons | Medium |
| 5th | Testimonial | Interview notes, verbal confirmations | Lowest |

**Golden rule:** Collect evidence as close to the source as possible. A system log beats a screenshot. A screenshot beats someone's memory of what happened. The most common audit failure is presenting documentary evidence (a policy) when system-generated evidence (a configuration) is needed.

sections.title: Types of Audit Evidence
sections.content:
Auditors collect evidence from four main categories. Each category has different strengths and weaknesses, and strong audits combine multiple types to build a complete picture. Relying on only one type of evidence is a common audit weakness that can lead to incorrect conclusions about control effectiveness.

**1. Documentary Evidence** — Written policies, procedures, standards, and guidelines. This category shows that controls are designed properly and that the organization has established the expected control framework. Examples: information security policy, access control policy, incident response plan, business continuity plan. The auditor tests: Is the document approved? Who approved it and when? Is it the current version? Is it accessible to the people who need to follow it? Documentary evidence answers the design question.

**2. Observational Evidence** — What the auditor sees or witnesses during the audit. This is powerful because it cannot be fabricated — the auditor witnesses the control in real-time. Examples: access card readers working at the building entrance, security guards checking badges, a system administrator demonstrating the user provisioning process. The auditor must consider: Is this observation representative of normal operations, or has a special demonstration been arranged? Surprise observations carry more weight than scheduled ones.

**3. Analytical Evidence** — Analysis of data, trends, and correlations that indicate control effectiveness. This category is increasingly important as organizations generate more data. Examples: trend analysis of security incidents over 12 months showing decreasing severity, benchmark comparison of patch latency against industry peers, correlation between access review completion rates and unauthorized access incidents.

**4. Testimonial Evidence** — Statements from interviews with personnel. This is the weakest category but often provides context that other evidence cannot. Examples: interview with the CISO about security program direction, discussion with an IT operator about their daily security tasks. Critical rule: never rely solely on testimonial evidence — it must be corroborated with documentary, observational, or analytical evidence. An employee saying 'we review access quarterly' is not evidence that the review happens.

**Practical example — Testing AC-2 (Account Management):** An auditor tests whether account management procedures are effective. Documentary: reads the account management policy — it is approved and current. Analytical: runs a report of all active accounts versus active employees — finds a 3% mismatch. Observational: watches the help desk perform account creation — confirms they follow the procedure. Testimonial: interviews the manager about their quarterly access review process. Conclusion: control is designed and operating effectively with a minor finding on the 3% mismatch.

sections.title: Evidence Collection Methodology
sections.content:
A structured approach to evidence collection ensures completeness, consistency, and defensibility. Ad-hoc evidence collection — the scramble before an audit — inevitably misses gaps and creates inconsistencies that auditors notice. A systematic methodology produces better evidence with less overall effort.

**The Evidence Collection Process — five steps:**

**1. Plan what you need.** Before any evidence collection begins, map each control to the evidence that demonstrates its operation. Create an evidence request list organized by control framework clause. This planning phase prevents the common failure of collecting the wrong evidence and having to go back.

**2. Request evidence early.** Send the evidence request list 2-4 weeks before the audit. Include: what evidence is needed, the preferred format (system-generated log export, signed document, screenshot), the submission deadline, and a point of contact. Early requests give control owners time to gather evidence without disrupting their normal duties.

**3. Use sampling effectively.** When testing controls that operate on many items, use statistical sampling rather than testing every instance. Sample size depends on population size:
| Population Size | Sample Size | Notes |
|----------------|------------|-------|
| 10-50 | 5-8 | Test all if population is very small |
| 51-250 | 8-15 | Statistical significance improves |
| 251-1000 | 15-25 | Focus on high-risk items |
| 1000+ | 25-30 | Diminishing returns beyond 30 |

**4. Document everything.** For each evidence item, record the control ID and description, evidence description, source (system, person, document), date collected, collection method, and whether it supports or contradicts the control claim. This documentation is what makes the evidence defensible.

**5. Cross-reference findings to evidence.** Link each audit finding to the specific evidence that supports it. An audit finding without supporting evidence is just an opinion.

**Worked Example — ISO 27001 Surveillance Audit Evidence:** For ISO 27001 Clause 9.2 (Internal Audit): the internal audit report for the current year with findings and management responses, the audit schedule showing planned audits were conducted on time, auditor qualification records, and evidence of corrective actions from the previous audit. Each piece is filed with a unique reference number cross-referenced to the ISO clause. Collection method: system-generated from the audit management tool.

sections.title: Worked Example: Preparing for an ISO 27001 Surveillance Audit
sections.content:
Your company achieved ISO 27001 certification last year. The certificate is valid for three years with annual surveillance audits. These audits are less intensive than the initial certification but still require thorough preparation. The auditor will review a subset of controls, check that corrective actions from last year have been completed, and verify that the ISMS continues to operate effectively.

**Phase 1: Internal Scoping (8 weeks before the audit)** — Start early. Review last year's findings — have all nonconformities and observations been addressed with completed corrective actions? Identify any changes in the organization since the last audit: new systems, acquired companies, regulatory changes, staff turnover in key security roles. Update the Statement of Applicability if there have been any changes in control applicability.

**Phase 2: Evidence Preparation (6 weeks before)** — Create an evidence binder organized by ISO clause:
| ISO Clause | Evidence to Prepare |
|------------|-------------------|
| 4.1 Context | Updated business context document |
| 4.2 Stakeholders | Stakeholder register with requirements |
| 4.4 ISMS | ISMS scope document (signed by management) |
| 5.1 Leadership | Board minutes showing security reviews |
| 6.1 Risk Assessment | Updated risk register + treatment plan |
| 7.2 Competence | Training records for security team |
| 8.2 Risk Treatment | Control implementation evidence |
| 9.1 Monitoring | KRI dashboard, incident logs |
| 9.2 Internal Audit | Internal audit report from past year |
| 9.3 Management Review | Management review meeting minutes |
| 10.1 Nonconformity | Corrective action records |

**Phase 3: Pre-Audit Walkthrough (2 weeks before)** — Conduct a practice audit with the internal team. Verify all evidence is accessible, complete, and properly filed. Identify gaps before the external auditor arrives.

**Phase 4: Audit Day** — Have a designated point person for each ISMS clause. Provide a dedicated room with evidence access. Take detailed notes on each auditor question. If the auditor identifies a finding, do not argue — accept it, document it, and plan corrective actions.

**Phase 5: Post-Audit Remediation (1-4 weeks after)** — Document all findings. Create corrective action plans with owners and deadlines. Submit remediation evidence to the certification body within the agreed timeframe.

**Key lesson:** The surveillance audit should not drive your annual evidence collection cycle. Organizations that maintain continuous audit readiness — collecting and filing evidence as part of normal operations throughout the year — find that the surveillance audit is a straightforward confirmation of ongoing compliance rather than a stressful event.

sections.title: Common Audit Evidence Pitfalls
sections.content:
Even experienced organizations fall into predictable traps when preparing audit evidence. Recognizing these pitfalls is essential for building an audit-ready culture.

**1. The great evidence scramble.** Everyone waits until 2-4 weeks before the audit, then panics. Policies are updated overnight without proper review. Logs are hastily collected. Screenshots are taken out of context to hide gaps. Fix: maintain a continuous evidence repository — collect and file evidence as part of normal operations. Set quarterly reminders for evidence collection.

**2. Stale screenshots.** A screenshot of a system configuration from six months ago might show a control that was changed five months ago. Fix: always include dates in evidence file names. Prefer system-generated audit logs over manual screenshots — logs are automatically timestamped.

**3. Approving your own access.** The IT manager approves their own administrative access review. This violates segregation of duties. Fix: enforce segregation — access reviews must be performed by the user's manager, not the user themselves.

**4. Excel-based evidence management.** Thirty spreadsheets with different naming conventions, stored in different shared drives. Fix: use a centralized audit management tool with standardized file naming, folder structure, and version control.

**5. Over-relying on policies.** A written policy proves intent, not operation. The auditor wants to see that the policy is actually followed. If the policy says 'passwords must be 12+ characters', show the Active Directory password policy configuration, not the policy document.

**6. Hiding exceptions.** Only presenting successful test cases and hiding exceptions erodes auditor trust. Fix: be transparent about exceptions and have a documented exception management process.

**The bottom line:** Good audit evidence practices are a year-round activity, not a pre-audit scramble. Organizations that are always audit ready have embedded evidence collection into daily operations.

sections.title: Evidence Preservation and Chain of Custody
sections.content:
In regulatory audits, legal proceedings, and data breach investigations, the integrity of evidence matters as much as the evidence itself. Evidence that cannot be proven authentic and unmodified may be rejected by auditors, regulators, or courts. Chain of custody — the documented history of evidence from collection through presentation — is the mechanism that establishes evidence integrity.

**Chain of Custody — the six Ws:** Every piece of evidence should be trackable through six questions. Who collected it? What is it exactly (file name, hash, description)? When was it collected (date and time)? Where was it stored (location, system, folder)? Why was it collected (which control does it support)? Who has accessed it since collection? A break in any link of this chain can invalidate the evidence.

**Evidence preservation best practices:** Store evidence in a dedicated, read-only repository. Use version control for documentary evidence. Hash forensic evidence using SHA-256 at collection time. Maintain access logs for the evidence repository. Define retention periods per evidence type.

**Retention periods by framework:**
| Framework | Evidence Retention |
|-----------|-------------------|
| ISO 27001 | At least one full audit cycle (3 years) |
| SOC 2 | 2 years minimum |
| PCI DSS | 3 years (12 months + current) |
| GDPR | Duration of processing + 3 years |

**Digital forensic considerations:** When evidence may be used in legal proceedings, use write-blockers when collecting from systems, hash all collected data immediately, document the collection methodology, and maintain an unbroken chain of custody log.

**Practical guidance for most GRC audits:** A well-organized evidence repository with clear folder structure, date-stamped files, and access logs is sufficient for the vast majority of audits. Full chain-of-custody procedures are needed for fraud investigations, data breach litigation, and regulatory enforcement actions.
